s1_b1: Two children on a merry-go-round. One near the center. One out at the edge.
s1_b3: The one at the edge sweeps a huge circle. The one near the center barely moves. So — different speeds.
s1_b4: But in another sense, they move together. Same start, same finish, same instant. They never separate.
s1_b5: Both stories are true. We just need the right way to measure turning — not distance along the ground, but angle swept.
s1_b6: How much angle you've turned through: angular displacement.
s1_b7: How fast that angle grows: angular velocity. Measured the same for every point on the disk.
s1_b8: Speed it up — the angle grows faster and faster. That change is angular acceleration.
s1_b9: Notice the shape of this. Position becomes angle. Velocity becomes rate of angle. Acceleration becomes rate of that rate. The same trio as straight-line motion, just turned in a circle.
s1_b10: And the bridge between the two worlds is the radius. Same angular speed, bigger radius, faster through space.
s1_b11: So the child at the edge really is faster — and yet they turn at the very same rate. No contradiction.
s1_b12: Angle, rate of angle, change of that rate. Computing angular velocity and acceleration, then converting them to linear speed through the radius — that's yours.
